original_abstract: Research into machine learning (ML) has seen an unprecedented surge in popularity over the last several decades. Despite this growth, researchers without a formal background in statistics often find it challenging to navigate the nuances of model evaluation and comparative analysis. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of the most common evaluation metrics across a wide range of supervised learning tasks, including binary, multi-class, and multi-label classification, as well as regression, image segmentation, object detection, and information retrieval. We provide practical guidance on selecting appropriate statistical tests, generating sufficient metric data for robust testing, and interpreting the resulting statistical significance. Finally, we demonstrate these concepts through real-world applications, comparing the performance of convolutional neural networks in classifying lung infections from X-ray imagery and detecting malignant tumors in positron emission tomography (PET) scans.
humanized_abstract: Introduction The popularity of research in machine learning (ML) has increased exponentially in the last few decades. However, a large number of researchers without a statistics background are not able to fully understand the methodology of evaluating ML models and their comparison. This survey provides an in-depth review of various evaluation metrics that are used to assess the performance of ML models for several supervised learning tasks such as binary, multi-class and multi-label classification, regression, image segmentation, object detection and information retrieval. It discusses the selection of statistical tests and provides insight into the generation of evaluation metrics for sufficient data to determine the statistical significance. The conducted experiments demonstrate the use cases for evaluating the performance of different types of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) models in assessing lung infections from X-ray images and malignant tumors from positron emission tomography (PET) scans.

It discusses the selection of statistical tests and provides insight into the generation of evaluation metrics for sufficient data to determine the statistical significance. The conducted experiments demonstrate the use cases for evaluating the performance of different types of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) models in assessing lung infections from X-ray images and malignant tumors from positron emission tomography (PET) scans.
